questions.prompt: Users report that `adds` is sometimes higher than the number of elements ever inserted. Why?

options.text: `HashSet.addAll` internally calls `add`, so elements added via `addAll` are counted twice by the overrides.
`adds` is not `volatile`, so increments are lost under concurrency.
`c.size()` includes duplicates that the set rejects.
`super.addAll` must be called before incrementing `adds`.

questions.explanation: This is the fragile base class problem: `HashSet.addAll` is implemented in terms of `add`, so each element increments `adds` in *both* overrides. The subclass silently depends on the base's private call graph. (b) concurrency would cause *undercounting*, not systematic overcounting. (c) duplicates would also make `adds` too high — but that's true of the composition version too and isn't what makes counts double per element; the defining, reliably reproducible bug is the self-call. (d) ordering doesn't change the double count.

questions.prompt: What is the most precise statement of what `class B extends A` couples B to, beyond A's public interface?

options.text: A's self-usage pattern — which of its methods call which others — even though it is undocumented and can change in any release.
A's private field names, which B must not shadow.
A's package, since subclasses must live alongside their parents.
Nothing extra — inheritance and composition couple to exactly the same surface.

questions.explanation: Overriding a method that the base also calls internally means B's correctness depends on *when and whether* A makes those self-calls — an implementation detail. That's the extra surface composition avoids. (b) shadowing is legal (if confusing) and not a contract. (c) false; subclasses can live anywhere the class is visible and non-final. (d) is exactly the claim the fragile-base-class problem disproves.

questions.prompt: Which scenario is the strongest *legitimate* use of implementation inheritance?

options.text: Extending a framework base class explicitly designed and documented for subclassing, with defined hook methods (e.g. a template-method lifecycle class).
Extending a third-party utility class to add one convenience method.
Extending `ArrayList` so a domain class can be iterated directly.
Creating `Manager extends Employee` to model job roles.

questions.explanation: Inheritance shines when the base is *designed for extension*: documented self-use, stable hooks, and subclassing as the intended API (servlets, widgets, template methods). (b) couples you to code you don't control — one library update can break you. (c) leaks 60+ list methods into a domain type; implement `Iterable` and compose. (d) roles change and combine — a person can be both — so the relationship isn't permanent; model role as data or composition.

questions.prompt: Which are real costs of the composition/decorator approach that a balanced design review should acknowledge? Select all that apply.

options.text: Forwarding boilerplate for every delegated method (in languages without delegation support).
The wrapper fails `instanceof`/identity checks against the concrete wrapped type.
The self problem: the inner object's internal self-calls and self-registrations bypass the wrapper's behavior.
Composition prevents the wrapped object from being tested in isolation.

questions.explanation: (a) is the day-one annoyance — Java makes you write forwarders; Kotlin's `by` removes most of it. (b) and (c) are the subtle ones: a `CachingRepository` wrapping `SqlRepository` is not a `SqlRepository`, and if the inner object passes `this` to a callback or calls its own methods, the wrapper's added behavior is skipped. (d) is backwards — composition *improves* testability, since the inner object is reachable through its interface and easily substituted.

questions.prompt: You need `Repository` behavior plus audit logging, and later probably caching and retry too, in varying combinations per deployment. Why is the decorator shape (`AuditedRepository implements Repository` wrapping a `Repository`) better than `AuditedRepository extends SqlRepository`?

options.text: Decorators run faster because they avoid virtual dispatch.
Concerns stack and combine at runtime over any `Repository` implementation, without coupling to `SqlRepository`'s internals or exploding the class hierarchy.
Subclassing is deprecated in modern Java.
Decorators do not require interfaces, reducing the number of types.

questions.explanation: With inheritance you'd need a subclass per combination (`AuditedCachingRetryingSqlRepository`…) and each couples to the concrete base. Decorators compose: wrap any `Repository` — SQL, in-memory, a mock — in any order, chosen at wiring time. (a) decorators add a delegation hop; they are not faster. (c) obviously false. (d) backwards — the shared interface is precisely what makes decorators work.

questions.prompt: "Use inheritance when B is-a A" fails as a design test because…

options.text: is-a relationships in the domain don't guarantee behavioral substitutability in code — a square is-a rectangle, yet Square extends Rectangle breaks Rectangle's contract.
is-a can only be verified at runtime.
modern languages no longer support inheritance.
is-a applies to interfaces but never to classes.

questions.explanation: The domain-language test checks vocabulary, not contracts. Code inheritance demands *substitutability*: every guarantee A makes, B must keep. Squares can't honor 'width and height vary independently,' so the geometric is-a doesn't survive translation into a mutable class hierarchy. (b), (c), (d) are simply false.
